text:
Team GB starAdam Peatywas consoled by aBBCreporter after missing out on yet anotherOlympicgold medal by just 0.02 seconds.

After a fairly slow start to the 100m breaststroke final in Paris, Peaty powered into contention in the final 50 meters as he sensed athird consecutive Olympic gold medal.

That would have seen Peaty, 29, become only the second man after American legend Michael Phelps to win the same swimming event at three successive Olympics.

A competitive and thrilling final all came down to the final stroke, with Italy’s Nicolo Martinenghi finishing just 0.02 seconds ahead of joint-silver medallists Peaty and American Nic Fink to clinch a shock gold.

While Peaty was unable to make more history in the pool and take home a third gold medal, he did secure a sixth Olympic medal after returning to the sport following a break due to mental health issues and alcoholism.

Peaty initially appeared in good spirits in an interview withBBC Sportstraight after the final but began to choke up as he reflected on his emotional and inspiring journey.

TheParis 2024 silver medallistsaid: ‘It has been a very long way back. I look at the score there and you would never think in your wildest dreams that it would be 59 seconds to win it.

“In my heart I’ve already won” â¤ï¸Adam Peaty let all his emotions out after he won silver in the men’s breaststroke final ð¥We’re very proud of you Adam!#BBCOlympics#Olympics#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/JxUFvunn2Z

‘I gave it my absolute all. I executed it as well as I could. It’s not about the end goal, it’s about the process. It doesn’t matter what time it says on the scoreboard, I think in my heart I have already won.

‘I’m so happy that I can race against the best in the world and still come joint-second. In my heart I have won, these are happy tears.

‘I gave my absolute best every single day and I cannot be upset about that. I’m almost an older man here now.

‘I can’t have that relentless pursuit every single day without a sacrifice of some sort – they come in every single form. It’s very hard to compete with the younger ones, so I’m very happy with that.’

Peaty continued to look emotional as he hugged his wife and young child in the stands after coming so close to another Olympic gold.

Analysing Peaty’s performance in the final, former freestyle world swimming champion Mark Foster said on BBC One: ‘Adam mentioned that he was one or two percent off – that is the difference between winning and losing.

An incredible journey back to the podium.Proud of you, Adam.#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/QU5tw5m2fa

‘You could pick it to pieces and think: “He could’ve done this, he could’ve done that on the evening” – and we could pick everyone else to pieces – but it is about who manages their emotions best and gets the best result.

‘But that was his best result for tonight.’

Double Olympic champion Rebecca Adlington added: ‘You could see Adam was genuinely happy. You could see they were happy tears.

‘I’m just emotional for him because, as an athlete, you know what he has been through. I have achieved nothing compared to Adam – what he has achieved is unbelievable – I can’t even get my head around it.

‘He is such a role model for the rest of that team – people look up to him and he just has so much respect. It is amazing to see him smile.’

Team GB remain without a gold medal at the Paris Olympics but Peaty’s silver takes them to four overall – two silvers and two bronze after two days of action in the French capital.

title: Team GB star Adam Peaty consoled by BBC reporter after Olympics agony | Metro News
description: Team GB star Adam Peaty was consoled by a BBC reporter after missing out on another Olympics gold medal by just 0.02 seconds.
